question: How does a type-safe DI container track types of registered services?

options.text: Through generic parameter accumulation: each register() extends TRegistry via intersection type
Through Map<string, unknown> with runtime checks
options.explanation: Map<string, unknown> is runtime storage. Type safety is provided by the generic parameter that extends with each registration

options.text: Through decorators that add type metadata
options.explanation: Decorators are a separate mechanism. Generic parameter accumulation works without decorators and provides compile-time checking

options.text: Through a JSON schema describing all service types
options.explanation: JSON schema is a runtime approach. The generic parameter works purely at the type level, without additional code

question: How does InjectionToken differ from a string key for identifying services?

options.text: Token carries a phantom type linking it to the service type
Each token is a unique object, eliminating name collisions
Token is faster than string in Map lookups
options.explanation: Map performance is the same for strings and objects. Token advantages are type safety and uniqueness, not speed

options.text: Token allows linking primitive values (string, number) with a typed key

question: What is a captive dependency and why is it a problem?

options.text: Singleton depending on a scoped service: the scoped instance is captured and doesn't refresh when a new scope is created
Circular dependency between two singletons
options.explanation: Circular dependency is a separate problem. Captive dependency occurs when the lifetime rule is violated: a long-lived service captures a short-lived one

options.text: A service registered twice under different names
options.explanation: Duplicate registration is a different problem. Captive dependency is a lifetime mismatch between dependencies

options.text: A dependency that isn't used by any service
options.explanation: An unused dependency is dead code, not a captive dependency. Captive is a lifetime problem, not a usage problem

question: How does an auto-wiring container detect circular dependencies?

options.text: Through a Set of currently resolving services: if a service is already in the Set, it's a cycle
Through static dependency graph analysis at registration
options.explanation: Static analysis at registration is more complex and doesn't cover conditional dependencies. Recursive checking at resolve is simpler and more reliable

options.text: Through a catch block for StackOverflow
options.explanation: StackOverflow is an uncontrolled error. Explicit checking via Set provides an informative message with cycle path

options.text: Through limiting recursion depth
options.explanation: Depth limiting can give false positives for deep but non-cyclic graphs. Resolving Set is a precise method

question: Which dependency lifetime rule is correct?

options.text: Singleton can only depend on singleton; scoped on scoped or singleton; transient on anything
Any service can depend on any other regardless of lifetime
options.explanation: This leads to captive dependencies: singleton will capture a scoped/transient instance and use an outdated version

options.text: Transient cannot depend on singleton as it violates isolation
options.explanation: Transient can safely depend on singleton — singleton lives longer, which doesn't create problems

options.text: Scoped can depend on transient but not on singleton
options.explanation: Scoped can safely depend on singleton (lives longer). Dependency on transient is also fine — transient is recreated on each resolve
